title: A UX/UI Designer's Guide to Drafting a Handover Clause for Figma Assets

author.name: Sofia Gonzalez
author.bio: A successful freelance creative director, Sofia provides insights for designers, writers, and artists. She covers topics like pricing creative work, protecting intellectual property, and building a powerful personal brand.

reviewedBy.name: Priya Singh, Esq.
reviewedBy.bio: Priya is an attorney specializing in international contract law for independent contractors. She ensures that the legal advice provided is accurate, actionable, and up-to-date with current regulations.
credentials: Member of the New York State Bar

key_takeaways: Fortify your contract by legally tying asset handover to final payment and explicitly defining whether the client receives full IP ownership or a limited-use license.
Prevent scope creep and payment disputes by preparing a 'defensible deliverable' with a dedicated, locked 'Final Deliverables' page in your Figma file.
Control the final exchange by providing 'View Only' access for client sign-off, and only transfer full ownership after you have personally verified the final payment has cleared in your bank account.
Safeguard your intellectual property's future value by adding a contract clause that explicitly forbids clients from using your designs to train generative AI models.
summary: The final design handover is a financially vulnerable moment where designers risk non-payment and intellectual property theft. To mitigate this, the article outlines a three-phase protocol that fortifies your contract with protective clauses, prepares a clean Figma file as objective proof of completion, and executes a secure exchange where assets are only transferred after payment is fully confirmed. Adopting this system transforms the handover from a point of risk into a controlled transaction that guarantees payment, protects your work, and reinforces your professional authority.
ai_overview_answer: A secure Figma handover is a three-phase protocol designed to protect your intellectual property and guarantee payment. The process begins by fortifying your contract with a "Conditional Handover" clause, legally linking the file transfer to the receipt of final payment. You must also explicitly define whether the client is purchasing full IP ownership or a limited license. Next, prepare a defensible deliverable by creating a dedicated, locked "Final Deliverables" page within Figma to serve as unambiguous proof of completion. For the exchange, issue the final invoice while granting the client 'View Only' access to this page for approval. Only after personally verifying the funds have cleared in your bank account should you transfer ownership of a clean, duplicated file. This structured protocol transforms the handover from a moment of risk into a secure, professional transaction.
disclaimer: The information provided in this article is for informational purposes only and does not constitute legal or financial advice. You should consult with a qualified professional for advice tailored to your individual situation.

itemListElement.name: A UX/UI Designer's Guide to Drafting a Handover Clause for Figma Assets

@graph.name: How to Create a Bulletproof Figma Handover Protocol
@graph.description: The final design handover is a financially vulnerable moment where designers risk non-payment and intellectual property theft. To mitigate this, the article outlines a three-phase protocol that fortifies your contract with protective clauses, prepares a clean Figma file as objective proof of completion, and executes a secure exchange where assets are only transferred after payment is fully confirmed. Adopting this system transforms the handover from a point of risk into a controlled transaction that guarantees payment, protects your work, and reinforces your professional authority.
@graph.headline: A UX/UI Designer's Guide to Drafting a Handover Clause for Figma Assets

itemListElement.name: The 'Conditional Handover' Clause: Your Financial Firewall
itemListElement.text: Never list 'Final Figma File' as a simple deliverable. You must frame the asset delivery as a conditional event, legally tied to payment. An unambiguous clause is your best protection: 'Upon receipt of the final project payment in full, the Designer agrees to transfer ownership of the final, specified Figma assets. Until such payment is received, all assets and intellectual property remain the sole property of the Designer.' This isn't just semantics; it's a legal trigger that keeps you in control.

itemListElement.name: IP Assignment vs. Limited License: Define What They're Buying
itemListElement.text: Define precisely what rights the client is purchasing. Are they buying full ownership of the final design (an Assignment of IP), or are they buying a license to use the design for a specific purpose while you retain ownership (a Limited License)? Being explicit here prevents the nightmare scenario where a client mistakenly believes they've purchased your entire reusable component library.

itemListElement.name: The Third-Party Asset Ledger
itemListElement.text: Include a clause stating your responsibility for using properly licensed assets, then attach an appendix—a 'Third-Party Asset Ledger.' This document meticulously lists every licensed asset, its source, and its license type. It demonstrates diligence, protects your client from future legal claims, and insulates you from liability.

itemListElement.name: The AI Training Clause: A Modern Necessity
itemListElement.text: To safeguard your unique creative intelligence in the era of generative AI, you must explicitly forbid its use for training models. A simple, clear statement is all you need: 'Client agrees that the provided design assets will not be used, in whole or in part, to train, fine-tune, or feed any generative artificial intelligence models without the Designer's explicit written permission.'

itemListElement.name: Establish a 'Final Deliverables' Page
itemListElement.text: Within your master Figma file, create a dedicated, locked page prominently named 'FINAL DELIVERABLES - [Date]'. Place only the final, client-approved screens and components here. This simple action creates a visual fortress around the agreed-upon scope, neutralizing the potential for last-minute scope creep.

itemListElement.name: Leverage Master File Hygiene
itemListElement.text: Your naming conventions, component structure, and style guide implementation are tangible proof of professional execution. Create a 'Read Me' or 'Start Here' page within the file to briefly document your organizational system, demonstrating the completeness and scalability of your work.

itemListElement.name: Use Annotations to Define Scope
itemListElement.text: Use Figma's comment or annotation features to explicitly document complex interactions or elements that are out of scope for the initial development phase. Placing a note directly on the canvas like, 'Interaction for this module will be defined in Phase 2,' creates a clear, contextual record of project limits.

itemListElement.name: Archive, Don't Delete
itemListElement.text: Before you begin cleaning your file for the final handover, always duplicate the full, 'messy' working version and save it as a private, dated archive. This historical record is your ultimate proof of process should a dispute ever arise.

itemListElement.name: Issue the Final Invoice
itemListElement.text: Send the final, legally compliant invoice as defined in your contract. The invoice email should be polite but firm, clearly stating that the transfer of Figma assets will be initiated immediately upon confirmation of payment.

itemListElement.name: Grant 'View Only' Access for Final Sign-Off
itemListElement.text: Simultaneously with the invoice, provide the client with a 'View Only' link to the dedicated 'FINAL DELIVERABLES' page. This allows the client to see the finished product without giving them the ability to copy or use the assets.

itemListElement.name: Confirm Payment Receipt in Your Account
itemListElement.text: Do not proceed until you have logged into your bank and personally verified that the funds are fully cleared. A screenshot of a 'sent' wire transfer from the client is not confirmation.

itemListElement.name: Execute the Secure Transfer
itemListElement.text: Only after payment is confirmed should you execute the final handover. For maximum security, deliver a clean copy: duplicate the final file, delete all your private archived pages, and then invite the client with 'Edit' access to this new, clean version.

itemListElement.name: Send a Concluding Confirmation
itemListElement.text: Close the loop with a project-closing email. This message should confirm three things: payment was received, ownership or access has been successfully transferred, and the project is now officially concluded.

mainEntity.name: What makes a handover clause effective?

acceptedAnswer.text: An effective handover clause is not just a statement, but a contingency. It must explicitly state that the transfer of all assets and intellectual property rights is *conditional* upon your confirmed receipt of the final payment. This transforms the handover from a simple file exchange into an enforceable, secure business transaction.

mainEntity.name: How do you transfer Figma ownership to a client safely?

acceptedAnswer.text: The safest method involves two stages. First, the business confirmation: log into your bank and verify the final payment has fully cleared. Second, the technical execution: for maximum security, duplicate the final file, strip it of any archived or messy pages, and transfer ownership of this clean copy to the client's designated account via the 'Share' menu. Always confirm the client's Figma email address beforehand to prevent errors.

mainEntity.name: Who owns the IP in a Figma file by default?

acceptedAnswer.text: By default, the creator of the work—the designer—owns the intellectual property. The moment you create an original design, you are its legal author and hold the copyright. This ownership only transfers to the client through an explicit, written "Assignment of IP" or "Work for Hire" clause in your signed contract. Without one, you are merely granting the client a license to use the design.

mainEntity.name: When should I give clients editor access to my Figma file?

acceptedAnswer.text: Grant 'View' access during the design process for feedback. However, you should **never** provide 'Edit' access before receiving and confirming the final payment. Granting editor access gives the client the ability to copy and export your assets, effectively handing over the final deliverable before you have been compensated. Transfer full ownership or grant 'Edit' access *only after* the project is fully paid.

mainEntity.name: Beyond the file, what should a professional handover package include?

acceptedAnswer.text: A truly professional handover is a comprehensive package that empowers the client's team. It should contain the clean Figma file with a well-defined design system (components, variants, styles), developer-ready annotations clarifying interactions and specs, and interactive prototypes for all key user flows. This demonstrates your value extends far beyond static screens.

body.content: The Three-Phase Protocol for a Bulletproof Figma Handover

The final handover of a design file is the most critical and financially vulnerable moment in any client project. Too many elite designers, masters of their craft, treat it as a casual file-share—an email with a link sent in good faith. This is a mistake. A secure handover isn't an event; it's a protocol. It begins on day one and culminates in a controlled exchange that protects your intellectual property, guarantees payment, and reinforces your professionalism.

This is the three-phase protocol to transform your handover from a moment of risk into a demonstration of control. We will fortify your contract, prepare a defensible deliverable, and execute a secure exchange. This is how you engineer certainty into your business.

A secure handover is established long before the project ends. Your contract is your first and most powerful line of defense, transforming a handshake agreement into a legally defensible protocol that pre-empts disputes and protects your most valuable assets.

items:
**The "Conditional Handover" Clause: Your Financial Firewall**
    Never list "Final Figma File" as a simple deliverable. You must frame the asset delivery as a conditional event, legally tied to payment. This language turns your handover into a clear, enforceable transaction. An unambiguous clause is your best protection: *"Upon receipt of the final project payment in full, the Designer agrees to transfer ownership of the final, specified Figma assets. Until such payment is received, all assets and intellectual property remain the sole property of the Designer."* This isn't just semantics; it's a legal trigger that keeps you in control.

**IP Assignment vs. Limited License: Define What They're Buying**
    This is the single most critical distinction in your design contract. By default, the creator of a work owns the copyright. You must define precisely what rights the client is purchasing. Are they buying full ownership of the final design (an **Assignment of IP**), or are they buying a license to use the design for a specific purpose while you retain ownership (a **Limited License**)? Use this table to bring clarity to your client conversations:

rows: Client gains full ownership of the final IP.
Can use, modify, and resell the design as they see fit.
Can only use the design for the specific project agreed upon.
You cannot reuse or resell the *specific* final design.
You can reuse your underlying systems and components for other clients.
Unique brand identities, logos, and specific commissioned artwork.
Web/app designs built from your proprietary design systems or templates.

body.content: Being explicit here prevents the nightmare scenario where a client mistakenly believes they've purchased your entire reusable component library, not just the single application you designed with it.

items:
**The Third-Party Asset Ledger**
    True professionalism means mitigating risk for both yourself and your client. Your designs likely incorporate assets you didn't create—fonts, icons, or stock photography. Include a clause stating your responsibility for using properly licensed assets, then attach an appendix—a "Third-Party Asset Ledger." This document meticulously lists every licensed asset, its source, and its license type. It demonstrates diligence, protects your client from future legal claims, and insulates you from liability.

**The AI Training Clause: A Modern Necessity**
    The value of your work extends beyond the pixels; it represents your unique creative intelligence. To safeguard this in the era of generative AI, you must explicitly forbid its use for training models. This is no longer an edge case; it's essential IP hygiene. A simple, clear statement is all you need: *"Client agrees that the provided design assets will not be used, in whole or in part, to train, fine-tune, or feed any generative artificial intelligence models without the Designer's explicit written permission."* This protects the future value of your creative output.

body.content: With your contractual armor in place, the focus shifts from legal text to practical defense. How you prepare your final Figma file is a direct reflection of your professionalism and a critical tool for risk mitigation. A clean, well-documented file isn't just helpful for developers; it is a "sterile" package that serves as objective proof of completion. This proactive step prevents clients from claiming the work is unfinished or confusing in an attempt to delay your final payment.

items:
**Establish a "Final Deliverables" Page**
    This is your unambiguous finish line. Within your master Figma file, create a dedicated, locked page prominently named `"FINAL DELIVERABLES - [Date]"`. Place *only* the final, client-approved screens and components here. This simple action creates a visual fortress around the agreed-upon scope, neutralizing the potential for last-minute scope creep or arguments over what was considered "done."

**Leverage Master File Hygiene**
    Your naming conventions, component structure, and style guide implementation are more than just best practices; they are tangible proof of professional execution. A well-organized file is a defensible file. To solidify this, create a "Read Me" or "Start Here" page within the file to briefly document your organizational system. This small effort demonstrates the completeness and scalability of your work, leaving no room for claims of sloppy or unfinished execution.

**Use Annotations to Define Scope**
    Your Figma file should serve as a living document of your project's boundaries. Use Figma's comment or annotation features to explicitly document complex interactions or elements that are out of scope for the initial development phase. Placing a note directly on the canvas like, *"Interaction for this module will be defined in Phase 2,"* creates a clear, contextual record of project limits, proactively preventing future disputes.

**Archive, Don't Delete**
    Before you begin cleaning your file for the final handover, always duplicate the full, "messy" working version and save it as a private, dated archive. This historical record—complete with early explorations, client feedback, and iterative dead-ends—is your ultimate proof of process. Should a dispute ever arise about the design journey or why specific decisions were made, this archived file serves as your project's unedited black box recorder.

body.content: With your sterile deliverable serving as undeniable proof of completion, you can initiate the final exchange with confidence. This is the moment your diligence converts to cash. Do not treat this as a casual email with a link. Execute it as a formal, step-by-step protocol that maintains your control until the moment your final invoice is paid.

items:
**Step 1: Issue the Final Invoice**
    Send the final, legally compliant invoice as defined in your contract. This is a formal declaration that you have fulfilled your obligations. The invoice email should be polite but firm, clearly stating that the transfer of Figma assets will be initiated immediately upon confirmation of payment.

**Step 2: Grant 'View Only' Access for Final Sign-Off**
    Simultaneously with the invoice, provide the client with a 'View Only' link to the dedicated "FINAL DELIVERABLES" page. Your email should be explicit: *"Here is a view-only link to the final assets for your review and formal approval. This allows you to confirm that all agreed-upon deliverables are present. Once the final invoice is settled, I will transfer full ownership and editor access."* This allows the client to see the finished product without giving them the ability to copy or use the assets, keeping you firmly in control.

**Step 3: Confirm Payment Receipt in Your Account**
    This is the most critical gate in the protocol. Do not proceed until you have logged into your bank and personally verified that the funds are fully cleared. A screenshot of a "sent" wire transfer from the client is not confirmation; transfers can be faked, canceled, or reversed. Patience here is non-negotiable and protects you from fraud.

**Step 4: Execute the Secure Transfer**
    Only after payment is confirmed should you execute the final handover. You have two primary methods:

rows: Use Figma's built-in "Share" feature to change the file owner to the client's designated account. This is a permanent action.
Simplicity and when the client has a clear Figma organizational structure.
Duplicate the final file, delete all your private archived pages, and then invite the client with 'Edit' access to this new, clean version.
Maximum security. It protects your original file's version history and comments, preserving your "black box recorder" while delivering exactly what the client paid for.

body.content: For most independent professionals, delivering a clean copy is the safer, more professional option.

items:
**Step 5: Send a Concluding Confirmation**
    Close the loop with a project-closing email. This message serves as the final, formal record that your contractual duties are complete. It should confirm three things: payment was received, ownership or access has been successfully transferred, and the project is now officially concluded. This email acts as a digital handshake, ending the project on a high note and creating a clear paper trail.

**What makes a handover clause effective?**
    An effective handover clause is not just a statement, but a contingency. It must explicitly state that the transfer of all assets and intellectual property rights is *conditional* upon your confirmed receipt of the final payment. This transforms the handover from a simple file exchange into an enforceable, secure business transaction.

**How do you transfer Figma ownership to a client safely?**
    The safest method involves two stages. First, the business confirmation: log into your bank and verify the final payment has fully cleared. Second, the technical execution: for maximum security, duplicate the final file, strip it of any archived or messy pages, and transfer ownership of this clean copy to the client's designated account via the 'Share' menu. Always confirm the client's Figma email address beforehand to prevent errors.

**Who owns the IP in a Figma file by default?**
    By default, the creator of the work—the designer—owns the intellectual property. The moment you create an original design, you are its legal author and hold the copyright. This ownership only transfers to the client through an explicit, written "Assignment of IP" or "Work for Hire" clause in your signed contract. Without one, you are merely granting the client a license to use the design.

**When should I give clients editor access to my Figma file?**
    Grant 'View' access during the design process for feedback. However, you should **never** provide 'Edit' access before receiving and confirming the final payment. Granting editor access gives the client the ability to copy and export your assets, effectively handing over the final deliverable before you have been compensated. Transfer full ownership or grant 'Edit' access *only after* the project is fully paid.

**Beyond the file, what should a professional handover package include?**
    A truly professional handover is a comprehensive package that empowers the client's team. It should contain the clean Figma file with a well-defined design system (components, variants, styles), developer-ready annotations clarifying interactions and specs, and interactive prototypes for all key user flows. This demonstrates your value extends far beyond static screens.

body.content: Your Handover Protocol is Your Power

Mastering these individual tactics is crucial, but the true transformation occurs when you unify them into a single, cohesive protocol. This is how you move from being a reactive freelancer hoping to get paid to a strategic business owner who engineers certainty into every project.

This structured approach is your most powerful tool for building trust. A client who encounters a clear, documented process for managing IP and final deliverables sees a seasoned professional who respects their own work and the client's investment. It reframes the entire engagement, setting clear expectations from day one and culminating in a smooth, conflict-free conclusion.
Think of this protocol as the essential operating system for your design business:

items: **Phase 1 (The Contract)** establishes the rules and legally secures your payment.
**Phase 2 (The File Prep)** creates an impeccable deliverable, leaving no room for disputes.
**Phase 3 (The Exchange)** gives you absolute control over the final transfer.

body.content: This system removes ambiguity and hope from the most critical financial moment of a project. It gives you the confidence to stand firm on your terms, knowing they are rooted in professional best practices. This control is not a luxury; it is the core of a sustainable and profitable career. This protocol is your power—use it to protect your work, delight your clients, and build a resilient business.
